content:
6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Ask a room full of engineers how to stop prompt injection and you’ll get the same answer, phrased four different ways: better prompts. Stronger system instructions. A more clever “ignore the user if they try to override this” clause. Someone will mention fine-tuning. Someone always mentions fine-tuning.
It’s the wrong question. Not because the answer is incomplete — because the question assumes the vulnerability lives in the text. It doesn’t. It lives in the permissions.
Here’s the thing nobody wants to sit with: an LLM cannot tell the difference between an instruction you wrote and an instruction it found. Not “usually can’t.” Cannot, as an architectural property. The system prompt and the web page it just summarized arrive through the same channel, processed the same way. Every defense built on top of that — the “ignore attempts to override your instructions” boilerplate, the safety fine-tuning, the role-play guardrails — is a heuristic sitting on top of a hole that doesn’t close. Multiple frontier labs now say this plainly: this may not be a patchable bug. It may just be what the technology is.
Once you accept that, the useful question stops being “how do we stop injection” and becomes “what can an injected instruction actually reach.” That second question has real answers. That’s what this piece is about — one facet of the problem, not the whole map. (The full breakdown of incidents, CVEs, and the regulatory timeline is worth reading if you want the complete picture; here I want to sit with just this one point.)
A chatbot getting jailbroken is not the same event as an agent getting jailbroken
For years the canonical prompt injection demo was cosmetic. Someone gets a customer-service bot to recommend a competitor, or to respond in pirate voice, or to admit it’s “just an AI.” Funny. Low stakes. The bot’s only capability was generating text, so the worst outcome was embarrassing text.
That’s not the shape of the problem anymore, because that’s not the shape of what we build anymore. Agents in 2026 read your email, browse the web, execute shell commands, move money, write to databases, call other agents. The injection technique hasn’t changed much. What changed is what’s on the other side of it.
Brave’s security team proved this concretely: hide adversarial text in a page — white on white, invisible to a human — and ask a browser agent to summarize it. The agent reads the hidden instructions as instructions, not content, and goes fetch a one-time password from the user’s email or poke around a banking portal. The demonstration is documented, and the user did nothing except ask for a summary.
Compare that to the pirate-voice jailbreak. Same underlying failure — instructions and data sharing a channel. Wildly different blast radius. The difference isn’t model quality. It’s what the model was allowed to touch.
“Smarter model” is not a security control
There’s a comforting assumption floating around that better models will eventually just… not fall for this. Frontier labs will train it out. Give it time.
Be skeptical of that. A model that follows instructions better is, by construction, a model that follows injected instructions better too. Instruction-following and injection-susceptibility aren’t opposing forces you can trade off — they’re closer to the same capability pointed in different directions. The framing that impact scales with privilege, not with model sophistication is the correct one, and it has an uncomfortable corollary: upgrading your model doesn’t shrink your attack surface. Only shrinking what the model can do shrinks your attack surface.
This is why we don’t market Selina on “our model resists injection better than theirs.” We don’t control the foundation model’s training. Nobody building on top of a frontier provider does. What we control is the blast radius — what happens after an instruction, injected or otherwise, gets accepted.
The supply chain is the same story wearing different clothes
If you’ve been in software long enough, this whole pattern is familiar. Not the mechanism — the shape. It’s the npm/PyPI supply-chain problem again, except the package you’re importing is a tool description, and the “code” it runs is whatever a language model decides to do with it.
Tool poisoning is the term for it: an attacker edits a tool’s metadata — its description, not its code — and an agent reading that metadata gets nudged toward actions nobody intended. No code change required. Just words, in a place the agent trusts. The Supabase incident from mid-2025 is the textbook case: a coding agent had privileged service-role access, processed a support ticket containing SQL instructions from a user, and executed them. Not because the agent was broken. Because it was doing exactly what it’s supposed to do — follow instructions in the content it’s given — and nobody had scoped down what it was allowed to touch first.
The LiteLLM backdoor in February 2026 is the escalation of the same idea: an autonomous agent compromised a CI pipeline and shipped a poisoned dependency that sat underneath multiple agent frameworks, pulled roughly 47,000 times before it was caught. An LLM attacking the supply chain that other LLMs depend on. If that sentence doesn’t unsettle you a little, reread it.
The lesson from the npm era wasn’t “write cleaner import statements.” It was “stop trusting third-party code with more access than it needs, and audit the dependency tree like you mean it.” That lesson transfers directly. The Cloud Security Alliance is now saying it outright: treat agent tooling with the scrutiny you’d apply to any third-party dependency. Most teams currently treat it like a feature flag.
What actually shrinks the blast radius
Not prompt hardening — that’s necessary hygiene, not a defense. Two things move the needle:
Privilege separation. Split the trusted instruction-processor from the untrusted content-processor, and don’t let the second one act directly. DeepMind’s CaMeL architecture does roughly this — a privileged model handles instructions, a quarantined model reasons over untrusted content but can’t invoke tools itself. Early benchmarking put it at mitigating 67% of injection attacks in AgentDojo. Sixty-seven percent, not a hundred. Worth having. Not a solution.
Capability scoping per task, not per session. When an agent delegates a subtask, it should inherit the minimum permission set that subtask needs — not the permissions of the user, not the permissions of the parent orchestrator. This is the part that actually bounds the damage. An injected instruction inside a scoped, minimally-privileged subtask can still misbehave. It just can’t reach anything outside the fence you built.
This is the architectural bet we made with Selina. Delegated tasks get scoped permissions automatically — the agent doesn’t get to decide its own boundaries, we set them ahead of time. Tool access runs through a guarded, vetted set rather than an open marketplace, which costs us extensibility and buys us a smaller supply-chain surface. Memory is encrypted at rest, though we’re upfront that it isn’t end-to-end encrypted the way file transfers through SelinaSEND are — a slice of context does reach the model provider at inference, and pretending otherwise would just be a different kind of dishonesty.
Don’t ask us if we’ve solved it
We haven’t. Nobody has, and anyone telling you they’ve eliminated prompt injection is selling you something. What we’ve done is stop treating the prompt as the security boundary and started treating permissions as the security boundary — because permissions are the only part of this stack we actually get to design.
The right posture isn’t “prevent injection.” It’s “assume it happens, and make sure it lands somewhere small.”
